condition.qualitative_description: Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or at least one severe week combined with four or more moderate weeks, indicates a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress and yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

Latest kharif seasonal rainfall is below 75% of the long-term mean annual precipitation, and the multi-year annual precipitation trend is declining, confirming a structural rainfall deficit rather than a single anomalous year.
Farmers report well or borewell deepening over 5–10 years, or seasonal failure of shallow wells, indicating co-occurring groundwater depletion alongside surface irrigation failure.
Surface water body total area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure.
Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.
Mean cropping intensity at or below 1.15 with no upward trend over the observation period confirms persistent single-season rainfed farming with no expansion of irrigated double-cropping.

No canal irrigation infrastructure intersects the micro-watershed AND fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works have been recorded, jointly confirming absence of both formal surface irrigation and community-level irrigation investment.

Many pending or rejected FRA claims with reported NTFP decline and forest conversion indicate tenure insecurity confirming encroachment vulnerability; high recognition weakens encroachment as primary driver.
More than 50 hectares of cumulative deforestation recorded in the micro-watershed, representing a significant absolute loss of forest habitat.

Farmers report moderate-to-high borewell/tubewell density (roughly 10–50 or more than 50 within 2–3 km) in hard rock terrain, indicating localised over-extraction not captured by block-level SOGE. Very low density or widespread borewell failure may indicate aquifer limits rather than density-driven stress.
Farmers report salty/brackish well water, white crust on soil or pipes, or water unsuitable for sensitive crops — indicating salinity from deep penetration or over-extraction.

Farmers report moderate-to-high borewell/tubewell density (roughly 10–50 or more than 50 within 2–3 km) in alluvium aquifers, indicating localised over-extraction not captured by block-level SOGE. Very low density or widespread borewell failure may indicate aquifer limits rather than density-driven stress.
Farmers report salty/brackish well water, white crust on soil or pipes, or water unsuitable for sensitive crops — indicating salinity from deep penetration or over-extraction.
Both the linear trend and the multi-year mean of annual groundwater recharge (P - ET - Runoff) are negative, indicating chronic depletion rather than a single drought year anomaly.
Dominant aquifer class is crystalline basement with less than 20% alluvial lithology, confirming low recharge capacity and high vulnerability to depletion.
Farmers report salty/brackish well water, white crust on soil or pipes, or water unsuitable for sensitive crops — indicating salinity from deep penetration or over-extraction.
Farmers report borewell or dug-well deepening over recent years, or seasonal failure of shallow wells during rabi or zaid, indicating declining static water levels.

Farmers report moderate-to-high borewell/tubewell density (roughly 10–50 or more than 50 within 2–3 km) in hard rock terrain, indicating localised over-extraction not captured by block-level SOGE. Very low density or widespread borewell failure may indicate aquifer limits rather than density-driven stress.
Farmers report salty/brackish well water, white crust on soil or pipes, or water unsuitable for sensitive crops — indicating salinity from deep penetration or over-extraction.
Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or co-occurrence of at least one severe week with four or more moderate weeks, indicates sustained crop-season water deficit.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

condition.expression: mean_kharif_precipitation < 0.75 * mean_annual_precipitation_mm or mean_annual_precipitation_mm < 700 or trend_annual_precipitation_mm < -5
condition.qualitative_description: Kharif seasonal rainfall below 75% of the long-term annual mean, or mean annual precipitation below 700 mm, or a declining trend exceeding 5 mm/year confirms structural precipitation deficit underpinning drought risk.

SPI at or below -1.0 (moderate meteorological drought), MAI below 0.5 (soil moisture inadequacy), and VCI below 35 (significant vegetation stress) together confirm concurrent meteorological and agricultural drought per India Drought Manual classification.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

Mean annual precipitation below 1000 mm (lower bound of AER-18 normal range of 900–1400 mm) or current kharif seasonal rainfall below 65% of the long-term kharif mean indicates a below-normal rainfall year consistent with meteorological drought.

Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or a combination of at least one severe week and four or more moderate weeks, indicates a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress and yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal precipitation below 75% of the long-run kharif normal, or mean annual precipitation below 1000 mm with a declining trend exceeding 10 mm/year, confirms a rainfall deficit context consistent with recurrent drought.

Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or a combination of at least one severe week and four or more moderate weeks, indicates persistent crop-season water deficit sufficient to cause yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture stress during critical crop growth stages.

Mean annual precipitation below 950 mm, or kharif seasonal precipitation below 700 mm in the latest year, or a declining trend exceeding 10 mm/year with mean below 1100 mm, indicates structural rainfall deficit in this sub-humid zone.

Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or at least one severe week combined with four or more moderate drought weeks, indicates a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress and yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% of historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal rainfall below 75% of the long-term annual mean, or a declining multi-year precipitation trend below 900 mm/year, confirms meteorological drought as the primary driver of water scarcity.

Three or more severe drought weeks, or six or more moderate drought weeks in the most recent kharif season, indicate persistent crop-season water deficit sufficient to cause yield loss in rice and other kharif crops.

Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with less than 50% historical rainfall) in the most recent kharif season indicate prolonged intra-seasonal moisture gaps that disrupt critical crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal rainfall in the most recent year below 75% of the long-term annual mean, or mean annual precipitation below 1200 mm, indicates a below-normal rainfall year consistent with meteorological drought in AER-13.

Three or more severe drought weeks, or six or more moderate drought weeks, in the most recent kharif season indicate a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress in an otherwise humid zone.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the most recent kharif season indicate a prolonged moisture deficit disrupting crop growth stages.

Monsoon onset is later than the earliest year on record AND kharif seasonal precipitation in the most recent year is below 80% of the multi-year mean, jointly indicating a delayed and deficient monsoon.
Three or more severe drought weeks, or five or more moderate drought weeks in the most recent kharif season, indicate persistent crop-season water deficit sufficient to cause yield loss.

A declining multi-year precipitation trend (>5 mm/year reduction) combined with a below-normal kharif season rainfall (less than 75% of expected kharif share) confirms a structural rainfall deficit driving drought.
Two or more severe drought weeks, or four or more moderate drought weeks in the latest kharif season, indicate meaningful crop-season water deficit even in a perhumid zone.
Three or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture stress periods that disrupt crop growth stages.

Kharif seasonal precipitation below 75% of the long-run annual mean, or a declining precipitation trend exceeding 20mm/year combined with mean annual rainfall below 1800mm, signals structural drought risk in this perhumid zone.
Three or more severe drought weeks, or six or more moderate drought weeks in the latest kharif season, indicate significant meteorological drought stress even in a normally humid island ecoregion.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate a prolonged moisture deficit disrupting critical crop growth stages.
Two or more severe drought weeks, or four or more moderate drought weeks in the latest kharif season, indicate meaningful crop-season water deficit even in a normally humid zone.
Three or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate a prolonged intra-seasonal break that disrupts crop water supply.

Kharif seasonal precipitation in the latest year below 75% of the long-run kharif normal, or a declining trend in annual precipitation exceeding 20 mm/year, confirms a structural rainfall deficit underpinning drought.

Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or a combination of at least one severe week and four or more moderate weeks, indicates persistent crop-season water deficit sufficient to cause yield loss.
Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged rainfall gaps that disrupt critical crop growth stages.

Long-run mean annual precipitation below 600 mm combined with a kharif season receiving less than 75% of the long-run annual mean confirms a below-normal monsoon year in an already moisture-limited zone.
Two or more severe drought weeks, or four or more moderate drought weeks in the latest kharif season, indicate meaningful crop-season water deficit even in a perhumid zone.

A declining trend in annual precipitation exceeding 30 mm/year, or a kharif seasonal total below 75% of the long-term mean annual precipitation, confirms a below-normal rainfall year driving drought stress.
More than 75% of cropped area is under single kharif season only, indicating near-total dependence on monsoon rainfall with negligible irrigation-supported rabi or double cropping.

No irrigation canal intersects the micro-watershed and fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works have been constructed, confirming absence of both surface irrigation infrastructure and public investment in water access.
No canal irrigation infrastructure intersects the micro-watershed, and fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works have been recorded, confirming absence of both surface and publicly-invested irrigation.
More than 75% of cropped area is under single kharif season only, indicating near-total dependence on monsoon rainfall with negligible irrigation-supported double cropping.

No irrigation canal intersects the micro-watershed and fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works have been constructed, confirming absence of both formal surface irrigation and community-level irrigation investment.
More than 75% of cropped area is under single kharif season only, indicating near-total dependence on monsoon rainfall with negligible double-cropping.
No irrigation canal intersects the micro-watershed and fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works have been recorded, confirming absence of surface irrigation infrastructure.
Surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure.
Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the MWS, indicating historically low public investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

Farmers report that well or borewell depth has increased noticeably over recent years, or that pumping costs have risen substantially, suggesting declining groundwater levels that increase the cost and difficulty of lift irrigation — particularly for marginal farmers.
Surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure relative to irrigable command area.
Fewer than 10 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works completed under MGNREGA in the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.
Total surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure.
Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting maintenance.
Surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year, or the latest year area is below 85% of the multi-year mean, indicating siltation, structural failure, or reduced catchment inflows.
Fewer than 10 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the micro-watershed, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

Fewer than 3 surface water bodies in the MWS combined with cooperative distance exceeding 15 km, indicating both a structural infrastructure gap and limited access to institutional credit for irrigation investment.
Total surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct SWBs are present in the MWS, indicating contraction of storage infrastructure.
Fewer than 10 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

Farmers report that shallow dug wells or hand pumps used for supplemental irrigation have dried earlier in the season compared to previous years, or that pump operating costs have increased due to greater lift depth. This is not directly measurable from available data but is a key field indicator of groundwater supplemental irrigation failure in alluvial settings.
Surface water body area is declining at more than 2 ha/year and fewer than 5 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure relative to irrigable area.

Fewer than 10 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure relative to the irrigation needs of a hilly MWS.

Farmers report that open wells or shallow borewells run dry or yield insufficient water during the pre-monsoon months (March–May) or during extended dry spells within the kharif season, indicating that groundwater backup for irrigation is also unreliable.

Declining surface water body area (more than 2 ha/year loss) or very few small water bodies relative to the micro-watershed, indicating insufficient or shrinking storage infrastructure on these small island territories.
Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the micro-watershed, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.
Cumulative deforestation exceeds 50 ha and afforestation covers less than half the deforested area, indicating a net deficit in forest recovery.
Total deforested area in the micro-watershed exceeds 100 ha, representing a substantial absolute loss of NTFP habitat.
More than 50 hectares of cumulative deforestation recorded in the micro-watershed, representing substantial habitat loss for NTFP species.
Total deforested area exceeds 50 hectares, representing a substantial absolute loss of NTFP habitat in a micro-watershed context.
More than 50 hectares of cumulative deforestation recorded in the micro-watershed, representing a significant absolute loss of forest habitat.
Cumulative deforested area exceeds 50 ha, indicating substantial habitat loss for NTFP species within the micro-watershed.

Total deforested area exceeds 50 ha, representing a substantial absolute loss of NTFP habitat within the micro-watershed.
Cumulative deforestation exceeds 50 ha and afforestation covers less than half the deforested area, indicating a net forest deficit with weak recovery.
Cumulative deforested area exceeds 100 ha, representing substantial absolute habitat loss for NTFP species in the micro-watershed.

Scheduled Tribe population exceeds 25% of village population, indicating high dependence on forest resources and elevated risk of NTFP access loss from encroachment-related tenure disputes.

Scheduled Tribe population exceeds 25% of village population, indicating high dependence on forest resources and elevated vulnerability to encroachment-driven access loss.

Many pending or rejected FRA claims with reported NTFP decline and forest conversion indicate tenure insecurity confirming encroachment vulnerability; high recognition weakens encroachment as primary driver.
Absent or disputed forest boundary demarcation (no pillars/signage, active boundary disputes) is a precondition for encroachment; clear demarcation with no disputes weakens the pathway.
Cumulative forest-to-farm conversion has increased by more than 50 ha over the available record, indicating active encroachment of forest land for agriculture.
Village has a significant Scheduled Tribe population (>25%) coinciding with measurable forest-to-farm conversion, suggesting forest rights disputes or displacement of traditional NTFP collectors.
Both forest-to-farm conversion and settlement expansion are occurring simultaneously in non-plains terrain, indicating compound encroachment pressure on hilly forest landscapes.

Combined SC and ST population exceeds 35% of village population, indicating a high proportion of socially marginalised households with limited asset base and greater livelihood vulnerability.
Distance to nearest bank branch exceeds 5 km, indicating poor financial inclusion and likely reliance on informal moneylenders at high interest rates.
Combined SC and ST population share exceeds 40%, indicating a high proportion of socially marginalised households with limited asset base and greater dependence on degrading commons.
Mean cropping intensity at or below 1.15 across the available record indicates that small holders are not compensating for limited land area through double-cropping or multi-season cultivation.
Distance to nearest APMC market exceeding 30 km indicates that small holders face high transaction costs and are likely dependent on intermediaries for price discovery, reducing net farm income.
